content:
**Fed Holds Rates Steady in January Meeting, Signals Patience Amid Cooling Inflation**

**January 28, 2026 — Washington, D.C.**

The Federal Reserve’s Federal Open Market Committee (FOMC) voted today to leave the target federal funds rate unchanged at an upper bound of 4.75%, marking the fourth consecutive meeting with no adjustment to interest rates. This decision comes as inflation continues its gradual descent and labor market indicators signal steady, if modest, growth.

Chair Jerome Powell, addressing reporters after the meeting, emphasized the Fed’s commitment to its dual mandate. “While inflation has moderated over the past year, we remain vigilant. The Committee judges that holding the current target range remains appropriate to support continued progress toward our 2% inflation goal and maximum employment,” Powell stated.

The decision was widely anticipated by markets, with futures pricing in no change ahead of the meeting. Most FOMC members signaled in their updated projections that rate cuts could come later in 2026 if inflation continues to ease and economic conditions warrant additional support.

Analysts noted the Fed’s cautious tone. “The central bank is clearly in wait-and-see mode,” said Laura Chen, chief economist at MarketWatch Advisors. “They don’t want to cut too soon and risk reigniting inflation, but they’re also mindful not to keep policy too tight as growth slows.”

Looking ahead, the FOMC will continue to monitor data closely, reiterating that any shift in rates will be “data dependent.” The next scheduled meeting is set for March 2026.
